coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of aqueous carbon quantum dots using bibenzoimidazolyl derivative and their antitumor activity against breast cancer cell lines
coredata.dc:creator: Prasad, Kollur Shiva
Shruthi, Govindaraju
Shivamallu, Chandan
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using small organic molecules as carbon source is becoming more and more prominent over recent years due to their extensive applications. Herein, we report the synthesis of CQDs by carbonizing an organic molecule, 1,7′-Dimethyl-2′-propyl-1H,3′H-[2,5′]bibenzoimidazolyl (DPBI). The crystallinity of DPBI-based CQDs is confirmed by XRD results, where, the observed peak at 25.8° is attributed to the reflection plane (002). Further, the results from HRTEM studies revealed that the morphology of the particles were spherical in shape with an average size between 2 and 3 nm and interplanar spacing 0.329 nm between two lattice fringes. The biological activity of as-prepared CQDs has been tested with respect to its inherent antitumor property, where the CQDs exhibited significant anti-cancer property against breast cancer cell lines in vitro and also showed specificity of action against tumor cells in ex ovo chick choreoallentoic membrane (CAM), which was used as in vivo model to study differential action of CQDs, wherein they show least cytotoxicity to normal cells, which is concordant with the in vitro study carried out to analyze activity of CQDs on red blood cells (RBCs) and white blood cells (WBCs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
HRTEM
Anticancer property
CAM assay
Breast cancer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns revealing high crystallinity of DPBI-based CQDs at 2θ = 25.8°, corresponding to (002) reflection plane.
HRTEM image of DPBI-based CQDs showing interplanar distance between two lattice fringes.
XPS high resolution scan of the C1s region of DPBI-based CDQs.
Microscopic image showing MDA MB 231 cells. (a) Showing intact untreated control MDA MB 231 cells; (b) CQDs treated cells showing formation of Ghost cells.
Showing percentage cytotoxicity of CQDs and Paclitaxel against MDA MB 231 breast cancer cell lines.
Showing percent hemolysis of CQDs and Aspirin against (a) RBCs and (b) WBCs.

Images showing (a) MDA MB 231 grafted on the embryo surface using Whatman filter paper; (b) MDA MB 231 tumor in the embryo, showing increased vascularization; and (c) CAM treated with CQDs (25 μg/mL), leading to tumor dissolution with normal development of limbs.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of aqueous carbon quantum dots using bibenzoimidazolyl derivative and their antitumor activity against breast cancer cell lines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Group, Manipal Centre for Natural Sciences, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal, Karnataka 576 104, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Biotechnology & Bioinformatics, Faculty of Life Sciences, JSS Academy of Higher Education and Research (JSSHER), Mysuru 570 015, Karnataka, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: JSS Academy of Higher Education and Research (JSSHER)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using small organic molecules as carbon source is becoming more and more prominent over recent years due to their extensive applications. Herein, we report the synthesis of CQDs by carbonizing an organic molecule, 1,7′-Dimethyl-2′-propyl-1H,3′H-[2,5′]bibenzoimidazolyl (DPBI). The crystallinity of DPBI-based CQDs is confirmed by XRD results, where, the observed peak at 25.8° is attributed to the reflection plane (002). Further, the results from HRTEM studies revealed that the morphology of the particles were spherical in shape with an average size between 2 and 3 nm and interplanar spacing 0.329 nm between two lattice fringes. The biological activity of as-prepared CQDs has been tested with respect to its inherent antitumor property, where the CQDs exhibited significant anti-cancer property against breast cancer cell lines in vitro and also showed specificity of action against tumor cells in ex ovo chick choreoallentoic membrane (CAM), which was used as in vivo model to study differential action of CQDs, wherein they show least cytotoxicity to normal cells, which is concordant with the in vitro study carried out to analyze activity of CQDs on red blood cells (RBCs) and white blood cells (WBCs).

ce:section.ce:para: The unique properties of carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene and their derivatives has received much attention in the field of physics, chemistry and material science [1]. Especially, carbon quantum dots attracted researchers over recent years due to their potential applications in photovoltaic devices, photocatalysis and cellular imaging [2]. The low toxicity of CQDs makes them most superior than their counterparts [3].

CQDs have been obtained using carbon-based materials like nanodiamonds [4], graphite [5], carbon nanotubes [6], carbon soot [7], activated carbon [8] and graphite oxide [9]. Various approaches have been employed to synthesize CQDs, which includes, combustion/thermal treatments [10], electrochemical method [11], etc. Recent advances in the green synthetic approaches of CQDs made a mark in the field of nanoscience and technology [12]. Due to the excellent properties of CQDs like high luminescence properties and high surface area, they have been widely employed for energy and display applications [13]. In this work, we report the synthesis of CQDs from carbonizing DPBI molecule, a biologically important molecule, reported in our previous work [14] and characterized with the aid of various spectroscopic and analytical techniques. In addition, we also investigated the anti-cancer efficacy of as-obtained CDQs, against MDA MB 231 breast cancer cell lines. The cytotoxicity of these CQDs on normal eukaryotic cell lines was analyzed in the current study. The CQDs were also tested for their specificity of action in ex ovo chick choreoallentoic membrane (CAM) which was used as an in vivo model to study the differential action of CQDs.

Powder XRD was recorded on Bruker X-ray diffractometer using a Cu Kα (1.5406 Å) radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on JEOL microscope operating at 200 KV after casting a drop of sample dispersion in acetone over a 300 mesh carbon coated copper grids and subsequent air drying before analysis.

As-synthesized CQDs was investigated for their antitumor property against MDA MB 231 breast cancer cell lines. The MDA MB 231 cell lines grown in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) was treated with varied concentrations of CQDs ranging between 25 and 100 μg/mL and was allowed to stand for 12 h at 37 °C after which the cells were visualized under bright field microscope to analyze change in cell morphologies.

The CQDs treated cells were further subjected to cytotoxicity analysis by MTT assay using untreated cells as control, the cells were incubated for 4 h upon addition of MTT reagent, followed by cell disruption using lysis buffer, the cells were allowed to stand for 2 h and absorbance was measured at 570 nm. The degree of formation of purple formazan was quantified to analyze the percentage of cell death observed in treated cells in comparison to control cells.

Cytotoxicity of CQDs was analyzed using RBCs according to the protocol mentioned in our previous report [17]. The cytotoxicity of CQDs against nucleated cell was analyzed using WBC cells isolated through RBC lysis method [18]. Isolated WBCs were treated with CQDs at varied concentrations ranging between 25 and 100 μg/mL and were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h followed by analysis of percentage of live cells using MTT assay.

The antitumor property of as-prepared CQDs was analyzed using Chick CAM as a live model. The shell of three-day old chick embryos was opened carefully without causing any damage to the embryo (CAM), and the developing embryos were aseptically transferred into a transparent glass bowl with a polyvinyl suspension to support the CAM and prevent physical stress, the CAM was carefully covered using transparent polyvinyl sheet. The ex-ovo CAM was maintained at 37 °C with 60% humidity in the incubator until the development of limb was seen. After the limb development was seen, MDA MB 231 cells were grafted on the embryo surface using Whatman filter paper as scaffold. The CAM was allowed to grow after grafting until development of tumor was seen. Up on development of tumor, varied concentrations of CQDs ranging between 25 and 100 μg/mL were injected to the main vein/peripheral vein of CAM which then entered the circulatory system through heart. The CAM was further incubated for 24–48 h and later observed to check the changes in tumor morphology. The untreated CAMs were used as control and well-known anti-cancer drug Paclitaxel was used as positive control. After 48 h, the tumor was exercised from the CAM and was subject to microscopic observation to analyze the changes in cell morphology.

The solution of DPBI in distilled water (150 mg in 500 μL) was placed in air-tight glass vial and heated in autoclave at 220 °C for 3 h. The resulting mixture upon cooling to room temperature yielded a brownish yellow precipitate, indicating the formation CQDs. This colored precipitate was then washed with chloroform (10 mL × 3 times) to remove agglomerated particles and solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure to obtain brownish yellow DPBI-based CQDs.

The XPS measurements were performed to confirm the bonding states of DPBI-based CQDs. The existence of distinct peaks in the high resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3) at 284.9 eV, 285.4 eV, 287.3 eV and 289.1 eV corresponds to (CC), (CC and CH), (COH/COC) and (COOH) chemical environments, respectively, typical to CDQs [16] and these results are consistent with FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1, ESI).

The XPS measurements were performed to confirm the bonding states of DPBI-based CQDs. The existence of distinct peaks in the high resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3) at 284.9 eV, 285.4 eV, 287.3 eV and 289.1 eV corresponds to (CC), (CC and CH), (COH/COC) and (COOH) chemical environments, respectively, typical to CDQs [16] and these results are consistent with FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1, ESI).

Treatment of MDA MB 231 cell lines with CQDs resulted in cell lysis at all the four concentrations viz., 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 75 μg/mL, and 100 μg/mL, with the highest number of ghost cells seen upon cell lysis at 100 μg/mL. Microscopic observation of the cell lysis is showed in Fig. 4 .

The extent of cell lysis caused by all the four concentrations CQDs viz., 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL on MDA MB 231 exhibited high degree of cytotoxicity with 85, 91, 98 and 100% cytotoxicity, respectively, in comparison with the standard drug Paclitaxel, which showed inherent cytotoxicity of 81, 88, 95 and 100%, respectively at same concentrations as that of CQDs (Fig. 5 ) (Note: The cytotoxicity exhibited by DPBI molecule was negligible, compared to as-prepared CQDs and standard drug).

As depicted in Fig. 6 , CQDs displayed least toxicity on both RBCs and WBCs cells in comparison to Aspirin, with toxicity of 11.32% and 6.83% on RBCs and WBCs, respectively at highest concentration of 150 μg/mL, whereas standard drug Aspirin caused 12.45% hemolysis in RBCs and 11.25% toxicity on WBCs at concentration of 150 μg/mL, indicating that the CQDs are safe against normal eukaryotic cells.

The differential action of CQDs on tumor cells and normal cells is a desirable property which can be used further for targeted treatment of tumor, in treatment of various types of cancers. Treatment of tumor in CAM (Fig. 7a and b) using as-prepared CQDs resulted in dissolution of tumor without causing toxicity to the CAM, which was evident with normal development and progression of limbs and eye in the treated CAM (Fig. 7c). However, this intriguing phenomenon may be attributed to the altered composition of lipids in the cell membrane of the cancer cells. Since the cancer cells have very high degree of lipid content in their cell membrane in comparison to normal cells, it is quite natural that the CQDs have high affinity towards cancer cells in comparison to normal cells. This leads to degranulation of cancer cells due to pore formation. Hence, this might be the most likely reason behind the high degree of cytotoxicity exhibited by CQDs against tumor cells.

The results from above biological studies clearly indicates that the CQDs show no toxic effects on RBC, WBC and normal epithelial cells in Chick embryo. However, CQDs were found to act on tumor cells in vitro and on tumor developed ex ovo, which is evident by cell disruption and disappearance of tumor, respectively. Furthermore, this suggests that CQDs specifically act on tumor cells. From the above observations, it may be hypothesized that the CQDs act on cell membrane receptor present on tumor cells, which include nuclear antigens, tumor markers expressed on tumor cell surface and are solely present on tumor cell membrane. Thus, this might be the reason for cell disruption in tumor cells with no adverse effect on normal cells, in both in vitro and ex ovo methods. This hypothesis is in concordance with several previous reports where CQDs have been used for diagnosis of cancers through fluorescence imaging where CQDs tumor cell specificity has been extensively established. It is intriguing to note that the CQDs synthesized in the current study not only has the ability to specifically bind to cancer cells as established in the previously published reports [20], but also has the ability to hinder its proliferation and disrupt the cancer cells. It is indeed noteworthy to mention that to the best of our knowledge, there are limited reports on quantum dots and their application in cancer research [21]. In this regard, the present study could be considered as the first example, establishing the anticancer properties of CQDs not only through in vitro studies but also through ex ovo model.

To sum up, we present a new synthetic route for the preparation of CQDs using an organic molecule, 1,7′-Dimethyl-2′-propyl-1H,3′H-[2,5′]bibenzoimidazolyl (DPBI) by carbonization method. The spherical shape of as-obtained DPBI-based CQDs was revealed by HRTEM analysis. The high crystalline nature of DPBI-based GDQs was evidenced by XRD studies and further confirmed by HRTEM results. As-prepared CQDs show differential action on cells, with high specificity of action against the tumor cells with less cytotoxicity on normal host cells such as RBCs, WBCs and embryonic cells. This study clearly proves the ability of carbon dots to cure tumor through ex-ovo model. Thus, making CQDs as potential alternatives for cancer therapy.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
FT-IR spectrum of DPBI-based CQDs.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2019.01.001.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: KSP thankfully acknowledge the Director, Indian Institute of Science, Bengaluru for analytical facilities. CS is grateful for funding obtained from DST-SERB (YSS/2015/001135/LS(Ver-I)) and thankful to JSSAHER for providing laboratory facilities.
